posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Thierry Richoux farms the Dauvissat vines on Vincent’s behalf, and some of his old vine plantings contain a little César; I wouldn’t be surprised if the same was true of Vincent’s parcel.

posts.name: Mel Knox

posts.name: Scott Brunson
posts.cooked:
Funny guys…

Up to 10% (I think) of local variety, César, permitted in Irancy. I seem to remember from a previous visit that it adds some color and backbone to the PN since Irancy is so far north.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Scott Brunson:

Funny guys…

Up to 10% (I think) of local variety, César, permitted in Irancy. I seem to remember from a previous visit that it adds some color and backbone to the PN since Irancy is so far north.

That’s the upside. But it can be tricky to ripen and be structurally coarse.

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked: The 10% inclusion can be actually quite noticeable, since César is much darker and a lot more tannic than Pinot Noir. I’ve only had a few Irancys, but I do love the rusticity, grip and lean style of the best wines.

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
As I recall, there is another variety allowed called Tressot. When we imported Irancy in the late 70s it was permitted. Don’t know about now.

In warm years Irancy can be quite good. We brought in the '76 and '78 and those vintages were quite popular.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Earlier this year I served Thierry Richoux’s 2003 Irancy Veaupessiot blind to some Belgian friends after a Roumier Amoureuses. They thought it was a very serious Charmes-Chambertin.

It’s an inherently very exciting appellation.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
William Kelley:

Earlier this year I served Thierry Richoux’s 2003 Irancy Veaupessiot blind to some Belgian friends after a Roumier Amoureuses. They thought it was a very serious Charmes-Chambertin.

It’s an inherently very exciting appellation.

Fascinating. Never heard of it.

Can you make some recommendations?

You guys had me at rustic and structurally coarse.
I’ve only had Irancy from two producers myself; Benoit Cantin and Franck Givaudin.

posts.name: Mark C Johnson
posts.cooked: Thierry Richoux is the only producer I’ve had. Both Lyle Fass and Elden Selections import him in the USA. I’m enjoying the 2014 now, lean, lithe and a bit rustic. Irancy is a sort of “chablis” pinot, minerally and crisp. I’d like to try more producers but it’s not a “hot” area for importers.

I’m going to spend a few days there later this year or early next to visit everyone in the village.

Patrick Piuze was telling me about a retired guy who made great wines, I’ll need to dig into my notes to find the name.

posts.name: Drew Goin
posts.cooked:
I dunno anything specific about the area, but here’s an educational article:


Gargantuan Wine
“Guess What’s In Your Bourgogne Rouge…”
September 21, 2015


“…You’ll probably never see an Irancy; in part because there aren’t many, and in part because Irancy are strangely earthy, alien reds, to which few wine buyers will commit resources. Irancy are always red, and are Pinot Noir with bits of César — a wildly tannic, rare grape allowed only in the Yonne region — growing in Chablis-like Kimmeridgian limestone marls. César can constitute up to 10% of Irancy reds.”

“…As of 2008, a mere 10 ha remain of César. Very little exists. But you can be sure a négociant operation like Bouchard would quite likely have access to some! This doesn’t prevent some growers (Domaine et Vignoble de Flavigny-Alésia, Domaine Sorin-Coquard, Cave de la Tourelle) from bottling a pure César…an experience I would bravely sip once in the name of science.”

link_counts.title: Guess What’s In Your Bourgogne Rouge … – GARGANTUAN WINE.

posts.name: Morten Båtbukt
posts.cooked: There’s a yearly BIVB tasting in Oslo that I’ve attended a few times. On the one hand it’s rather obvious why quite a few of the producers present doesn’t have an importer, on the other one gets to taste a lot of small appellations that one rarely sees much of. There’s been a few Irancys of different quality,but generally at prefer the ones without Cesar (although Dauvissat’s is good, so if it has Cesar it disproves me) and the grape by itself seems to make wines with a brambly, rustic character structure wise, dark in colour and somewhat lacking infruit depth. I haven’t been a fan.

posts.name: J0zef P1nxten
posts.cooked:
A tasting note from a month ago:

Thierry Richoux Irancy 2009. Brought back from the domaine a week ago by my brother. Color very light red, nice evolution. On the nose, light red fruit, bit of pomegranate and meat. More in direction of Gevrey-Chambertin. On the palate, not super lengthy, but already nicely integrated tannins. Price quality excellent (around 15 Eur). Goes to show that you can find some treasures outside the cote d’or. Irancy of dauvissat family made by same producer.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: César is typically co-planted as I understand it - you don’t find plots growing apart.

There are some 100% césar wines. They smell terrible in most years - but I didn’t have to hold my nose for the 2015s ! The mid-palate flavour onward is quite interesting. César usually ripens up to 10 days later than the pinot, so blending the two is an ‘art’

For anyone who’s interested: Irancy

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
The 2015 Richoux Irancy is an outstanding QPR.

A rustic, earthy, stemmy, sappy fruited bottle of goodness. Almost a cross between a gamay and a pinot. Closes with a slight tincture of bitterness (iodine?) that is intriguing. Drinking this side by side with the 2018 Roilette Fleurie that I opened last night. This is better, I’d down-score the Roilette on this second night. The lower acid in this vintage was much more notable, especially next to this Irancy, which incidentally also comes from a rather ripe year for the region.

The 2015 Richoux Irancy is an outstanding QPR.

The lower acid in this vintage was much more notable, especially next to this Irancy, which incidentally also comes from a rather ripe year for the region.

Can you please PM me where you bought this? I’ve been looking for this wine since I tasted the 2010; which I bought on a road trip & cant recall the store’s name.

Thanks in advance!

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
The 2015 Richoux Irancy is an outstanding QPR.

A rustic, earthy, stemmy, sappy fruited bottle of goodness. Almost a cross between a gamay and a pinot. Closes with a slight tincture of bitterness (iodine?) that is intriguing. Drinking this side by side with the 2018 Roilette Fleurie that I opened last night. This is better, I’d down-score the Roilette on this second night. The lower acid in this vintage was much more notable, especially next to this Irancy, which incidentally also comes from a rather ripe year for the region.

The 2013 is also showing really well right now, btw. On my last visit, I bought bottles of the 2015 and magnums of the 2013 and am very pleased with both. In due course, look out for the lieu-dit bottlings. Thierry’s importer has some in 2014 and 2012 by the look of it: Thierry Richoux - The Source At some point, Thierry will be releasing a micro cuvée from vines trained “sur échalas” and cultivated by hand, which look like this:


Thierry Richoux in his Irancy vineyards by WilliamGFKelley, on Flickr

A recommendation specifically for you, Robert, given your affinity for what used to be called “French country wine”, would be William Charriat’s wines. They are the most old fashioned Irancy wines I have tasted, where the thick layers of tannin and extract are as much part of the flavor as the fruit. You can feel them cleaning out the arteries with every sip. The 2011s are very good. Weygandt imports.

Looking at a couple of other producers in the village, Cantin’s wines are lovely, and in a somewhat more polished style while retaining plenty of character. And a new small négoce operation is Maison de la Chapelle, which is owned by Grégory Viennois, Laroche’s winemaker: a more modern style, but very nicely done.

To give you a sense of why Irancy really doesn’t “follow the rules”, you can see in this photo what an amphitheater it is. So it very much has its own mesoclimate, and vintages such as e.g. 1998 and 2011 that let’s say have limitations elsewhere in Burgundy can end up being banner years for Irancy.


Irancy by WilliamGFKelley, on Flickr

The other main crop of the village was, historically, cherries; and you can tell wine is doing quite well just now, as there are fewer and fewer cherry trees left every time I visit. But the effect, at this time of year, is very picturesque, and I am sorry that I likely won’t be able to visit this Spring.


Irancy and cherries by WilliamGFKelley, on Flickr

link_counts.title: Thierry Richoux in his Irancy vineyards | WilliamGFKelley | Flickr

posts.cooked:
Scott Brunson:

Funny guys…

Up to 10% (I think) of local variety, César, permitted in Irancy. I seem to remember from a previous visit that it adds some color and backbone to the PN since Irancy is so far north.

That’s the upside. But it can be tricky to ripen and be structurally coarse.

And smells terrible if not fully ripe - it made a massive (+ve) difference to 2017 Irancy - so much more colour and body than the ‘pure’ pinot wines - to the young wines that had some included. I mention ‘young’ because Guilhem Goisot is definitely not a fan, because he says that it can be okay young, but that age brings absolutely nothing of value to césar wines.

Burgundy-Report – 26 Sep 05

03. The grapes of Burgundy

Ex-BIVB, 2018 – it doesn’t include Beaujolais, but it’s a pretty image! The truth is out – Burgundy wine is not just red, it can be white or rosé, it can even be fizzy. Prin…

posts.name: Scott Brunson
posts.cooked:
Scott Brunson:

Funny guys…

Up to 10% (I think) of local variety, César, permitted in Irancy. I seem to remember from a previous visit that it adds some color and backbone to the PN since Irancy is so far north.

That’s the upside. But it can be tricky to ripen and be structurally coarse.

And smells terrible if not fully ripe - it made a massive (+ve) difference to 2017 Irancy - so much more colour and body than the ‘pure’ pinot wines - to the young wines that had some included. I mention ‘young’ because Guilhem Goisot is definitely not a fan, because he says that it can be okay young, but that age brings absolutely nothing of value to césar wines.
03. The grapes of Burgundy – Burgundy-Report

posts.name: Lee Short
posts.cooked:
has anyone tasted the goisot les mazelots? i am very familiar with the whites but i can’t seem to recall ever trying the irancy.

I’ve only had the '15, but it was very impressive. Bought 4 bottles and drank them all in a couple of months.

has anyone tasted the goisot les mazelots? i am very familiar with the whites but i can’t seem to recall ever trying the irancy.

I’ve only had the '15, but it was very impressive. Bought 4 bottles and drank them all in a couple of months.

2018 - super
2017 - also super but with an obvious oak note 14 months ago
2016 - Irancy only, no Mazelots made, more modest, perfumed, smaller wine with more tannin showing than usual - this was the big hail, hence, just one cuvée
2015 - Less full-shaped than more recent vintages - racy, mineral, also a slug of oak showing when young
2014 - excellent
2012 - super

Irancy, easy to find in the US? Very spotty IMHO. I’ve always thought of it as a strong value French Bistro or Winebar wine. posts.name: Eric Zlotogura
posts.cooked:
Bumping up an old thread but this weekend I was in NYC at was glazing over a 24 page wine menu so the Somm came to help us out. He suggested a 2017 Richoux Irancy. At the price point there was not much of a risk so we agreed to give it a go.

I was just getting around to putting my thoughts into CT so I didn’t forget the bottle and stumbled on this older thread so I thought I’d bump it back up.

I am fairly new to Red Burgundy’s but regardless of the region, the fact that this is $26 retail is a bargain for a quality made good wine, period. On the nose very prominent floral and red fruits. The taste was less fruit forward and more mineral/earth driven but in a balanced well. I’d place it more in the medium body/medium finish area. Lastly, and probably most importantly, it paired well with our food and we finished 60-70% of the bottle during the appetizers it was that good! My wife is usually a 1 glass gal but she had her 2nd glass for sure she liked it so much.

Given what is posted up thread and what I’ve read online, I’ll probably be on the hunt for more finds from this region as it seems to be a bit under the radar and likely producing some quality products.

posts.cooked: I bought it quite a few months ago, if not a year ago from Zachy’s. I believe it was discounted or something as I rarely buy from Zachy’s, buy it was a quick and easy transaction.

posts.name: Peter Chiu
posts.cooked:
The 2015 Richoux Irancy is an outstanding QPR.

A rustic, earthy, stemmy, sappy fruited bottle of goodness. Almost a cross between a gamay and a pinot. Closes with a slight tincture of bitterness (iodine?) that is intriguing. Drinking this side by side with the 2018 Roilette Fleurie that I opened last night. This is better, I’d down-score the Roilette on this second night. The lower acid in this vintage was much more notable, especially next to this Irancy, which incidentally also comes from a rather ripe year for the region.

The 2013 is also showing really well right now, btw. On my last visit, I bought bottles of the 2015 and magnums of the 2013 and am very pleased with both. In due course, look out for the lieu-dit bottlings. Thierry’s importer has some in 2014 and 2012 by the look of it: Thierry Richoux - The Source At some point, Thierry will be releasing a micro cuvée from vines trained “sur échalas” and cultivated by hand, which look like this:


Thierry Richoux in his Irancy vineyards by WilliamGFKelley, on Flickr

A recommendation specifically for you, Robert, given your affinity for what used to be called “French country wine”, would be William Charriat’s wines. They are the most old fashioned Irancy wines I have tasted, where the thick layers of tannin and extract are as much part of the flavor as the fruit. You can feel them cleaning out the arteries with every sip. The 2011s are very good. Weygandt imports.

Looking at a couple of other producers in the village, Cantin’s wines are lovely, and in a somewhat more polished style while retaining plenty of character. And a new small négoce operation is Maison de la Chapelle, which is owned by Grégory Viennois, Laroche’s winemaker: a more modern style, but very nicely done.

To give you a sense of why Irancy really doesn’t “follow the rules”, you can see in this photo what an amphitheater it is. So it very much has its own mesoclimate, and vintages such as e.g. 1998 and 2011 that let’s say have limitations elsewhere in Burgundy can end up being banner years for Irancy.


Irancy by WilliamGFKelley, on Flickr



The other main crop of the village was, historically, cherries; and you can tell wine is doing quite well just now, as there are fewer and fewer cherry trees left every time I visit. But the effect, at this time of year, is very picturesque, and I am sorry that I likely won’t be able to visit this Spring.


Irancy and cherries by WilliamGFKelley, on Flickr

Merci …Sir William and Robert.

Very happy to report that the 2015 Irancy Les Cailles just released by SAQ, n Quebec, Canada at CAD49.75 per 750 ml.

posts.name: Lee Short
posts.cooked: Given the ripeness of 2015 in nearby regions, I’d guess that 2015 is a strong vintage in Irancy. Just a guess, though.

posts.name: Peter Chiu
posts.cooked:
Yes - I agree totally regarding the comments of a ripe vintage for 2015 red.

We are lucky to have this wine here as we (up-North) seldom get any break. I will buy for the following reasons : : (1) the price is excellent and just right as the price for red burgundy is going up through the roof for the last 12 to 16 months in Quebec, Canada; (2) according to Sarah K.- Thierry Richoux is Irancy; (3) 2016 is a natural low-yield and Richoux only produced one wine for vintage 2016 - out of the normal 4; (3) the general character of vintage year 2017 should not be as good as the general character vintage year 2015 - to match the style of Richoux wines; (4) I am not sure of anything regarding Richoux 2018…; plus (5) I just finished reading Page 190 of J.Morris - Inside Burgundy Edition 2.

That being said, it will be a first for me to have a red from near Chablis - which is too far north of Charmes-Chambertin and/or Gevrey-Chambert. I will cross my finger and hope I am right…



****Earlier this year I served Thierry Richoux’s 2003 Irancy Veaupessiot blind to some Belgian friends after a Roumier Amoureuses. They thought it was a very serious Charmes-Chambertin. ****

*Thierry Richoux Irancy 2009. Brought back from the domaine a week ago by my brother. Color very light red, nice evolution. On the nose, light red fruit, bit of pomegranate and meat. More in direction of Gevrey-Chambertin. On the palate, not super lengthy, but already nicely integrated tannins. Price quality excellent (around 15 Eur). Goes to show that you can find some treasures outside the cote d’or. Irancy of dauvissat family made by same producer.
I also really like David Renaud Irancy + David Renaud Irancy ‘les Mazelots’

posts.name: Peter Chiu
posts.cooked:
Robert…I ordered 26 bottles of Les Cailles 2015 on-line. It came in 6 bottles case.

As soon as I received this order, I opened one bottle. The aromas was expressive for about 15 minutes and then started to retreat just a little bite.The palate was structure but smooth with no rustic at all. I could sensed there were lots of stuffing hidden behind and wishing to jump-out - unlike any generic bourgogne.
I am sure it was the vintage 2015 was speaking to me. I know Richoux releases their wines 2 years late but now for 2015 …it is amazing. So without any hesitation, I load-up more cases of 2015 due FOMO.

As I am new to Irancy so my comments are not that reliable but one thing is very certain, the 2015 Richoux Irancy Les Cailles will developed into be a better wine that my purchase - years ago in 2008-2009 - of the 97 bottles of Audoin-Marsannay-La Charme aux Prêtres 05 (1119-8745 $37.50).

I need to sale my dog if the 2017 Veaupissiot is good as the 2015 Les Cailles.

posts.name: Mark C Johnson
posts.cooked: Ha. Is your dog that desirable? Good luck Peter with that. As for these Richoux Irancy, a few years may reward the patient. Be patient grasshopper.

posts.name: Peter Chiu
posts.cooked:
Merci William. Your comments will help to make my decision. So I will definitely add more than 2 bottles of 2017 for enjoyment and wait until 2025 to start drinking the 2015 earnestness.

SAQ has more than 500 bottles on-line. It is good news and I will keep any eyes on available stock and look for a discount.

posts.name: Peter Chiu
posts.cooked:
What a great thread. Thanks for the insights William, and Peter, thanks for flagging both of these as available at the SAQ. I’ve ordered three bottles of each one to pick-up when I next visit Montreal.

Just finished a Monday long Lunch event with the main theme event : What about Now - Is William Kelly ( & Alfred ) Right - About with Richoux Irancy ?

We were matching the following 3 - 2015 :

The Richoud Irancy Les Cailles 2015 with my 2 other ( my personal favorite ) 2015 namely : Dom.B.Glantenay - Santenots 15 ( 1237-9581$83) and Pavelot - S-L-B 15 (1350-7513 $ 41.50).

The 3 - 2015 were served pop and pour non-blind. As predicted, the Les Caillres and S-L-B were expressive right at the beginning and not holding back; whereas the Volnay 1er was not showing its usual self.

After 30 minutes all were singing - specially the S-L-B. It was the preferred bottle at this point.

After 60 minutes, with the foods, the S-L-B is beginning to show its lightness and other 2 were beginning to show the palates were more dense and intense. They were all singing and continue to be very expressive.


Starting from 90 minute to 120 minutes, the S-L-B is starting to fade whereas Irancy is getting closer to be like a Gervey 1er ( or if not at least a Gervey village ) and needless to say : Volnay 1er behaved like itself.

I was for a while, comparing the Irancy as a Pommard but at the end, I would really say it is more a Gervey 1er with its smooth texture and it *roundness - without rustiness *.

With more bottle-age and at age 10, I am certain the Irancy 2015 would really be more a Gervey 1er than a Pommard or Pommard 1er.

Conclusion, extremely impressive to its price - I mean for the Irancy Les Cailles 2015 by Richoux.

Brovo !!

posts.name: Peter Chiu
posts.cooked:
I would like to keep an up-date. The whole stock of the on-line release ( + 500 bottles ) of Richoux 2017 Irancy - Veaupessiot has disappeared.

It seldom happened like this with SAQ. It is kind of weird because the 2015 Irancy - Les Cailles is still available for on-line with a stock of 33 bottles and also it is available at many SAQ Stores across the whole Province.

My guess is : the customer ( who bought all the bottle ) is from a restaurant/wine-bar chain.

A red 2017 should be drinking more beautiful, easily accessible than the same wine from 2015 within the next one to two years.

BTW…SAQ web indicated the 2015 Richoux - Irancy Les Cailles is rated : 93 by WA ( thanks to Sir William)

posts.name: Chris V a n d e r m a r e l
posts.cooked:
Had the pleasure of trying the first Irancy I can recall tonight, the prior mentioned Richoux Les Cailles 2015. @William_Kelley got this one right! Great value and great buy. So thrilled to have a few more of this and several other Richoux in the cellar.

  • 2015 Gabin et Felix Richoux Irancy Les Cailles - France, Burgundy, Côtes d'Auxerre, Irancy (10/9/2022)
    This pours light ruby in the the glass with flecks of pink. The nose is immediately expressive and complex showing red cherry, pomegranate, petrichor, wet mushroom covered forest floor, animal fur, a kiss of sweet cedar, a touch of rose, and hint of background dried ground ginger powder. The palate is lovely entering on the abundant and bright red fruit, turning to medium tannin and medium acid. The finish closes out on waves upon waves of gorgeous cherry fruit, closing on the whisps of dried ground ginger powder and forest undergrowth. The finish lingers impressively, quite long really. All in all here we have a gorgeous bottle of Red Burgundy, offering up some superb early drinking. This is my first bottle of Irancy that I can recall. I drink a lot of very cool climate Pinot Noir, yet to me this shows the warmth and weight of the Cote d'Or, which perhaps might be due to vintage. In fact if tasted blind I could see calling this a Morey, Gevrey or Nuits St George Villages from an excellent producer. Just stunning stuff that is worth every penny of it's pricetag and more. I'm positive this will age well for decades to come. William Kelley got this one right. (93 points)
posts.display_username: Chris V a n d e r m a r e l

posts.name: Max Semler
posts.cooked: FWIW, Sage Society has 2009 and 2010 Thierry Richoux Veaupessiot in stock. @William_Kelley or others have experience with these vintages?

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Yeah, they are actually comparatively recent releases. Both showing well, 2010 a bit more mature.

In general you have to remember that Irancy is a sheltered amphitheater of hills, and actually tends to make quite ripe, powerful wines despite its latitude. It captures the sun and heat, and on top of that the local culture favors relatively brawny styles, hence the traditional presence of César in the blend. Wines for Gauls, if you will…

If you’re looking for ethereality and perfume north of Dijon better to go to Epineuil than Irancy. Or Irancy in years such as 2021…
I recently tasted the 2018 Dauvissat (René & Vincent) Irancy at a friend’s house. It was quite lush and fruit driven, at a level that surprised me, reminding me more of a warm-climate Pinot Noir.

posts.name: Chris V a n d e r m a r e l
posts.cooked:
Popped a regular Irancy 2017 tonight. Loved this. @William_Kelley strikes again.

  • 2017 Gabin et Felix Richoux Irancy - France, Burgundy, Côtes d'Auxerre, Irancy (10/24/2022)
    This pours very light ruby in the glass with flecks of pink. The nose is expressive and appealing with only modest complexity today, but just a thoroughly enjoyable profile. Aromas are of red cherry, fresh strawberry, rose petals, peonies, thyme blossom, crushed rocks, white mushroom, and faint whiffs of citrus peel. The palate enters on the bright red fruit, turning to medium minus, almost low, tannin, with medium plus acid and medium finish replaying crushed rock, thyme blossom, and citrus peel. All in all this is my second time trying Richoux Irancy and this bottle exceeded my expectations. Again this is a ringer for a nice bottle from the Cote d'Or and I think could easily compete with (or embarrass) some villages or 1ers. Unlike the 2015 Les Cailles I had previously, this was significantly lighter in color and weight with much more elegant aromatics. If the 2015 Les Cailles was like a Gevrey, this was much more elegant ala Volnay, Chambolle, or Beaune. In fact, I think if tasted blind I'd guess a Beaune 1er from a good producer. Again, to my palate this is not to be confused with other cool climate Pinot Noir such as Germany, Ontario, Alsace, it is much more Cote d'Or. This is an outstanding bottle of wine, drinking nicely on the fruit right now, but I expect this to last decades and improve, potentially increasing in score. A great bottle and in the scheme or modern Burgundy pricing, a tremendous value at $37 CAD. (92 points)

posts.name: Todd French
posts.cooked:
Bumping this thread as I very much enjoyed my first taste of Irancy, in a @William_Kelley recommendation (through a Wine Advocate report/review) - 2019 Benoit Cantin Irancy Palotte.

I enjoyed half the bottle, putting the other half in a 375ml bottle, corked, refrigerated, to enjoy some days later, but initial impressions were entirely positive. Juicy, explosive fruit, beautiful texture, touch of earthiness - I enjoyed this thoroughly and look forward to exploring the other half of the bottle, see what a bit of ‘age’ and air does to it. I see no reason to age these, but WK says they do age well, so I might hold one or two back to test the theory.
